title: Big O, Ω, Θ, and Little o
subtitle: Upper bounds, lower bounds, and tight bounds

sections.heading: More than just 'how slow can it get'
sections.body:
**Big O** describes an upper bound: 'this algorithm takes *no more than* this long'.

But sometimes we need more precision:

**Big O** → *at most* this long (upper bound)
**Big Omega (Ω)** → *at least* this long (lower bound)
**Big Theta (Θ)** → *exactly* this long, up to constants (tight bound)
**little o** → *strictly less* than this (strict upper bound)

📊 **In practice**: You'll see Big O 90% of the time. The others matter when you're being precise — especially in research papers and interviews when the interviewer asks 'is your bound tight?'

data.text:
f(n) = O(g(n))    →  f grows AT MOST as fast as g       (upper bound)
  f(n) = Ω(g(n))    →  f grows AT LEAST as fast as g       (lower bound)
  f(n) = Θ(g(n))    →  f grows EXACTLY as fast as g        (tight bound)
  f(n) = o(g(n))    →  f grows STRICTLY slower than g     (strict upper)
  f(n) = ω(g(n))    →  f grows STRICTLY faster than g     (strict lower)

  Relationship:  Θ(g) = O(g) ∩ Ω(g)
                 o(g) ⊂ O(g)  but never equal

sections.heading: Understanding the notation
sections.body:
Before diving into formal definitions, let's clarify what the symbols mean:

**f(n)** = your algorithm's actual runtime (e.g., '3n² + 5n + 2 operations')
**g(n)** = a simpler reference function (e.g., 'n²' or 'n log n')
**c** = a constant multiplier (any positive number like 2, 10, 100)
**n₀** = a threshold input size ('for all inputs bigger than this')
**∃** = 'there exists' (at least one such value)

📝 **Example**: When we say f(n) = O(n²), we're saying:
'My algorithm f takes at most c·n² time for large enough inputs (n ≥ n₀)'

The constants c and n₀ let us ignore small inputs and constant factors — we only care about the growth rate as n gets huge.

sections.heading: Formal definitions
sections.body:
Each notation has a precise mathematical definition:

**Big O (upper bound)**
f = O(g) if ∃ c, n₀: f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for n ≥ n₀
*Translation*: f grows no faster than g (up to constants)

**Big Omega (lower bound)**
f = Ω(g) if ∃ c, n₀: f(n) ≥ c·g(n) for n ≥ n₀
*Translation*: f grows at least as fast as g

**Big Theta (tight bound)**
f = Θ(g) if f = O(g) **and** f = Ω(g)
*Translation*: f grows exactly as fast as g (same upper and lower bound)

**Little o (strict upper bound)**
f = o(g) if for every c > 0, ∃ n₀ such that f(n) < c·g(n) for n ≥ n₀
*Translation*: f grows strictly slower than g

💡 **Key insight**: The shift from O to little-o is the 'for every c' — the gap between f and g must keep growing, not stay constant.

steps.text: Linear search through an array of n items, looking for x. In the **worst case** (x not present), we scan all n.
Big O — upper bound: at most n comparisons. So linear search is **O(n)**.
Big Omega — lower bound: at minimum we always check at least 1 element. So it's **Ω(1)**. (Not very useful, but technically true.)
steps.highlight: Best-case time: Ω(1) — we get lucky on element 0

steps.text: Big Theta — only if upper and lower match. Since worst is O(n) and best is Ω(1), linear search is NOT Θ(n) on every input. It IS Θ(n) on the worst case specifically.
steps.highlight: Saying 'linear search is Θ(n)' is sloppy — clarify worst/best case.

steps.text: Little o: n² grows strictly faster than n, so n = o(n²). Linear search is o(n²) — easily — but that's a loose statement.

sections.heading: When does this matter in practice?
sections.body:
**Interviews**: when asked 'what's the complexity?', most people say 'O(n)' and mean Θ(n). If your bound is loose, an interviewer might ask 'can you make that tight?'

**Lower-bound proofs**: showing that ANY comparison-based sort must be Ω(n log n) — no algorithm can beat that. This is why we say merge sort is *optimal* among comparison sorts.

**Algorithm design**: when you prove an algorithm is Θ(n log n), you've also proven you can't make it faster than that *with this approach*.

questions.question: Which symbol expresses 'this algorithm takes at least this long'?
questions.explanation: Omega is the lower bound — it says no matter how clever you are, you can't go faster than this.

questions.question: If an algorithm is both O(n²) and Ω(n²), it is also...
questions.explanation: Theta is the intersection of O and Ω — same upper and lower bound means the bound is tight.

questions.question: Is f(n) = n = o(n²)?
options: Yes — n grows strictly slower than n²
questions.explanation: n/n² = 1/n → 0 as n grows, so n is strictly dominated by n². n = o(n²) is correct.

questions.question: Saying merge sort is 'Θ(n log n)' means:
options: It runs in n log n seconds
It always takes proportional to n log n time (up to constants)
Its best case is n log n, worst is something else
It can never be faster than n log n in any case
questions.explanation: Θ is tight — best, worst, and average are all asymptotically n log n.
